executive_summary: March 2026 paints a picture of a market where institutional and individual investors are living in different emotional neighborhoods. Institutional investors have fully embraced a buy-the-dip mentality — every single one surveyed expects a quick recovery after a sharp drop — yet only half of them think stocks will be higher a year from now. Individual investors, meanwhile, have turned notably pessimistic on the one-year outlook and pulled back sharply on buy-the-dip faith. Both groups remain uneasy about crash risk, and neither is particularly confident that stocks are fairly valued.

key_findings.headline: Individuals Grow More Doubtful
key_findings.interpretation: Only a third of individual investors expect prices to rise over the next year, down sharply from about 58% in February. Institutional investors held steady at a coin-flip 50%, suggesting cautious ambivalence rather than conviction in either direction.

key_findings.headline: Crash Fears Linger Unevenly
key_findings.interpretation: Individual crash confidence fell back to January's low of 20%, meaning most wealthy individual investors see a crash in the next six months as a real possibility. Institutional crash confidence dipped only slightly from February, with half of managers still feeling relatively safe.

key_findings.headline: Institutions All-In on Rebounds
key_findings.interpretation: Every institutional respondent in March expressed confidence that a sharp single-day drop would fully recover within two days — a striking unanimity. Individual investors moved in the opposite direction, with buy-the-dip faith falling from 81% to just 40%.

key_findings.headline: Overvaluation Worries Persist
key_findings.interpretation: Half of institutional investors and 40% of individuals believe stocks are not overvalued — a modest uptick for institutions from February's 31%, but still well below strong conviction. Of course, investors who subscribe to market efficiency would view this question differently, since the current price, by definition, already reflects all available information.

detailed_analysis:
The most striking feature of the March data is the dramatic split in buy-the-dip confidence. Institutional investors reached 100% — unanimous faith that a sharp one-day drop would bounce back within 48 hours. Individual investors went the other way, falling from 81% to 40%. It's as if professional managers and everyday wealthy investors are reading entirely different playbooks about how resilient the market is to sudden shocks.

On the one-year outlook, individual investors have become markedly more pessimistic. Only a third expect prices to be higher in twelve months, down from nearly 58% just a month ago. That's a significant swing in a short window. This could reflect reactions to specific economic data, growing unease about policy uncertainty, or simply the natural mood swings that come with volatile markets. Institutional investors, at exactly 50%, seem to be shrugging — not bullish, not bearish, just uncertain.

Crash anxiety tells its own story. Individual investors are back at January levels, with only 20% confident a crash won't happen — meaning four out of five see it as a plausible scenario. Institutional investors are somewhat calmer at 50%, but that's still a coin flip, not confidence. When half your professional money managers can't rule out a crash, the mood in the room may be more guarded than it appears on the surface.

Valuation confidence improved modestly for institutions — rising from about 31% to 50% — though it's worth noting that for investors who believe markets are generally efficient, the question of overvaluation is almost beside the point. Among individuals, 40% don't see overvaluation, which is actually an improvement from January's striking low of just 7%. Still, the majority in both groups either believe stocks are pricey or aren't sure — a backdrop that may help explain the muted enthusiasm elsewhere in the survey.
market_context: [PLACEHOLDER: Add 2-3 sentences about recent market news relevant to this month's readings — e.g. Fed decisions, major earnings, economic data, or geopolitical events.]
methodology: These indices come from the Yale/Shiller U.S. Stock Market Confidence Surveys, which have tracked investor sentiment since 1989. Each month, roughly 100 wealthy individual investors and 100 institutional investment managers answer the same standardized questions. The consistency of those questions over 35+ years is what makes the data meaningful — you can actually compare investor psychology across very different market environments. The four indices each measure something distinct: • 1-Year Confidence: The share who expect prices to be higher in 12 months. • Crash Confidence: The share who believe a crash in the next 6 months is very unlikely (under 10% probability). A low reading means most people think a crash is plausible. • Buy-on-Dips Confidence: The share who believe that after a 3% single-day market drop, prices would fully recover within two days. • Valuation Confidence: The share who do not believe the market is overvalued. Surveys were conducted semi-annually before 2001 and have been monthly since July 2001. Respondents rotate over time, but consistent characteristics — high income, investment experience — are maintained across the panel.
